title: Meet the Food System with Dr. Walter Willett

25 years
much of his work has focused on the

he has published over 2 000 research

great well thank you very much Smith and

and talk and
and interact about the food system and

this is really a very satisfying event

uh and uh wonderful people who put the

faculty staff at Harvard and a lot has
been done we've had some huge

it's not been all put together and it's

15 minutes but it just provide a brief

this graphic was put together by our

nicely looks at the overview of the 30

things like fertilizer seeds energy

that goes into our food system
uh then there's the actual production of
the foods on pastures until land and

fishing uh then there's an important
step here of processing and
Manufacturing foods
and oftentimes this is overdone as you

warehouses and then distribution to

here at Harvard there's a preparation a
little bit that's not left out here in
between this green arrow here that's
what the Food Service does and then we
have it on our plate that's the fork and
knives and consume the food but of
course it doesn't even stop there
there's a waste when the system of many
types but all along here
it's not just boxes these are people too
in fact huge numbers of people working
in all different kinds of roles here uh
the actual number of farmers is a small
percentage of the population of course
now it used to be the majority of
Americans have still less than two
percent at this point in time uh but in
every step there's people involved here
producing the food but also consuming
the food and there's also things
emanating from the food production and
food system uh greenhouse gas emissions
pollution lots of things that are
unwanted that are having huge

effects on humans as well so very
complicated system here in one of our

to have people understand various parts

together better
uh there's another view of the food
system here that I actually like called
coronary capitalism uh and the quote
here is a big agriculture gets paid for
growing the corn often subsidized by the
government and the food processors get
paid for adding tons of chemicals to
create a habit forming and that's
irresistible product
along the way scientists get paid for
finding just the right mix of salt sugar
and chemicals to make the latest instant
food maximally addictive advertisers get
paid for peddling it and in the end the
healthcare industry makes a fortune
treating the disease that inevitably
results and that's not just Karl Marx
speaking there that's a senior Harvard
Economist uh Professor Kenneth rogoff
who's actually former Chief Economist at
the international monetary fund which I
think is a pretty realistic description
of unfortunately what a lot of what's
happening in our food system not
everything
but and by the way there's a huge amount
of greenhouse gas emissions and the
system is devastating our planet right
at this point in time
so it's um
it's it's complicated and it's not
working very well for the health and
well-being but the problem is they're
all along this chain everybody's making
lots of money and that's that's a
serious problem there's huge vested
interest in the system the way it is
it's we can't just turn dials and change
it easily
uh the health implications are huge and
I think many people are aware of those
uh this is looking at the average body
mass index our measure of obesity it's
not perfect but it's actually remarkably
good and looking from 1975 uh what's
been going on over time this is men this
is women almost linear increase in body

the definition of obesity uh about 42
percent of Americans at this time and
unfortunately this is American

are the main high-income countries in

of the reasons for showing this slide is
it's bad but it's not inevitable because
you look at other countries
Japan being the most remarkable here in

have 42 but also some of the European

what's going on as something uh
inevitable
I and this is having huge Health
implications and that's what I study
most uh I could spend hours talking
about the harmful effects of obesity we
know cardiovascular disease diabetes but
cancer
eye disease almost every organ of the
body is adversely affected and not
surprisingly even before the pandemic
life expectancy which had been going up
for virtually since we started tracking
it was going down four years before the

give it another kit but uh you know
obesity epidemic has been an important
contributor and then of course covid

here that the African-American
population lost on average of about
three years of life expectancy see and
the Latino population last about five
years of life expectancy during that
time maybe some of that will recover but
it didn't show it during the last the
most recent data we have of course a lot
of this is due to uh
basic structural issues structural
racism uh poverty income disparities but
there are also some very direct effects
that obesity is a major risk factor for
dying from covid and so is diabetes
hypertension so there's some if we had
had if we had been in better metabolic
shape we probably would have had 30 to
50 percent fewer deaths in covet in this
country so the virus is the main factor
of course but when it affects the
population that's in bad metabolic shape
the consequences are multiplied
so the scale of the challenge that we
were given when I led the Atlantic
commission which was put together to
address this these problems and I'll
talk about some of the other problems
was uh already in this world uh many
people are still not adequately fed uh
in many countries around the world
particularly uh sub-Saharan Africa parts
of Asia there's still major childhood
under nutrition about two billion people
mostly children like iron and vitamin A
and adequate amounts we have about 155
million children stunted and then again
this huge number overweight or obesity
and that number is about a date now
it's also I think apparent to everybody

Environmental
uh disasters are increasing and the

Florida and it clearly climate change is

and more severe but what's really

that's fundamentally why the university
has made climate change
the top priority right at the moment
because if we can't deal with this
nothing else is going to matter
so that's a little background I and by

that diet to feed the world's population

so we're challenged at the moment and
then we have another two billion people
who will need to feed
so this these challenges are pretty

that we would Define a healthy diet by

randomized trials
then our earth science colleagues have

some strategies and
this uh the reason we're here is one of

um so I'm going to give just a little
brief flavor of the kind of evidence
we've what we looked at and the kind of

develop to understand best how diet

meta-analysis of 66 randomized Trials of
food food group effects on risk factors
for cardio metabolic disease like heart
disease diabetes so the risk factors
would be things like blood pressure high
blood glucose high cholesterol levels so
these kinds of randomized Trials are
sort of the quick uh relatively
inexpensive they're not cheap though
studies where you take a couple dozen
people you actually feed them give them
their food for a few weeks under control
conditions and then you measure changes
in their in these factors but lipids
blood glucose measurements blood
pressure pressure and inflammatory
factors those are all included here so
this that's not our ultimate endpoint
we're really interested most in
preventing disease but we know that
these factors predict more disease so
you can do short-term studies under
control conditions and then they rank
various food groups by their effect on
these risk factors and the ranking gave
not too surprisingly nuts the highest
rank followed by legumes whole grains
and fish
fruits vegetables so this is really the
basis sort of the core of a plant-based
diet here and the short-term studies
suggested this would be healthier then
we got into less healthy foods refined
grains red meat eggs dairy and worst of
all sugar sweetened beverages so
Coca-Cola is the winner here there's no
nutritional value and there's a lot of
harm
so with that gave us some suggestions uh
some evidence that those dietary factors
at the top would be best to be the
healthiest to be the core of our diet
but again we're wanting to look at
prevention of disease and promotion of
well-being so that kind of work requires
large populations and following people
for decades and that's what most of the
work our group has focused on for the
last now over 40 years
uh one example here is the nurses health
study where we enrolled about 121 000
women back in 1976 it was originally
focusing on all contraceptives and
health but I was interested in nutrition
I'm joined to next year and we started

body weight uh physical activity smoking

start with a serving a date and then

there's a lot of flexibility about how

beans and nuts but limiting red meat

vegetables in abundance and then fat is
not necessarily bad for us we consume

unsaturated fats soybean oil olive oil
uh canola oil healthy unsaturated fats
